title: Light Sources, Measurement, and Safety
narrationScript: Magnetic particle testing ends at your eyes. Every ampere you set and every bath check you run comes down to one moment, light striking an indication and the indication reaching your eye. So the standards treat light the way they treat amperage, as a controlled, measured variable with hard minimums. This lesson gives you the numbers, the instruments, and the safety habits. Start with visible particles. Black or red particles work by contrast against the surface, and contrast needs illumination. The common minimum is one hundred footcandles of white light at the surface being examined, which is roughly one thousand lux. At the surface is the part people miss. A shop ceiling light that feels bright can deliver a small fraction of that down in a fixture or inside a bore. Portable lamps, drop lights, and flood lamps make up the difference, positioned to kill glare. Fluorescent particles flip the logic. Instead of flooding the part with white light, we darken everything and excite the particles with ultraviolet light in the UV A band, wavelengths from about three hundred twenty to four hundred nanometers, centered near three hundred sixty five. The particles absorb that energy and glow yellow green, right where the human eye is most sensitive. The classic source is a mercury vapor spot lamp behind a deep violet filter. It needs several minutes to warm up to full output, it drifts with line voltage, and after a power interruption it must cool before it will restrike, so you verify it after it stabilizes. Newer LED ultraviolet lamps light instantly and run cooler, but their intensity can be uneven across the beam, so they are used per their certification and checked for beam coverage as well as peak intensity. The working minimum is one thousand microwatts per square centimeter of UV A intensity at the examination surface. And because the method depends on darkness, ambient white light in the viewing area is capped, commonly at two footcandles or less, which is about twenty lux. That means a real booth, a hooded bench, or a tented setup, genuinely dark and not merely dim. Your eyes need time to adjust to that darkness, so procedures build in an adaptation pause before you evaluate, commonly at least one minute, and experienced inspectors give it several. None of those numbers means anything without measurement. The ultraviolet meter is a radiometer reading microwatts per square centimeter. The white light meter is a photometer reading footcandles or lux. Both are calibrated instruments with due dates, commonly on a six month cycle, and both are read with the sensor at the examination surface, facing the lamp, at the distance and position where the work actually happens. When do you check? At the start of each shift or every eight hours is typical, plus any time a bulb is changed, the lamp is moved, or power is interrupted. A failed reading stops fluorescent evaluation until the cause is found, an aging bulb, a dirty filter, low line voltage, or simply the lamp sitting too far away. Now safety, because ultraviolet light is energy aimed near your face all day. UV A is the least hazardous ultraviolet band, but only while the lamp is intact. The filter on a mercury lamp does two jobs at once. It passes UV A and it blocks the shorter, harsher wavelengths underneath. A cracked or missing filter leaks UV B, and that lamp comes out of service immediately. Never stare into any ultraviolet source, and keep it aimed at the work, not at people. Bare skin under a strong beam for hours earns the same respect you give sunlight, so sleeves and gloves cover it. Your eyes will also fluoresce slightly. The fluid inside the eye glows under stray ultraviolet and the world goes hazy, which is one more reason inspectors wear ultraviolet absorbing spectacles. They block the reflected ultraviolet, they cut the haze, and they make faint indications easier to see, not harder. Mercury lamps also run hot, so give the housing respect when changing bulbs, and remember the restrike delay after a power blink. One last habit separates professionals from passengers. Log the readings. Lamp intensity, ambient light, meter serial numbers, due dates. When a customer or an auditor asks how you know an indication that faint was even visible that day, your answer is a recorded number from a calibrated meter, taken at the surface where the part actually sat. Treat light like an instrument. Measure it, record it, and protect the eyes that make the final call.
imagePrompt: An inspector wearing UV-blocking spectacles presses a digital radiometer sensor flat against a dark steel crankshaft journal inside a curtained booth, the lamp above washing the scene in violet light.

sections.heading: Light Is a Procedure Variable
sections.body:
Every MT examination ends as a visual act: light, indication, eye. Governing practices (ASTM E709/E1444 and ASME Section V Article 7 among them) therefore treat illumination exactly like amperage — a specified, measured, recorded variable. Two lighting regimes exist, one per particle family.

**White light for visible particles.** Black, red, or gray particles work by contrast, and contrast needs illumination: the common minimum is **100 fc (about 1,000 lux) of white light at the examination surface**. The qualifier carries the meaning — *at the surface*. General shop lighting that feels bright overhead routinely measures a fraction of the requirement down in a fixture, inside a bore, or under a bench hood. Sources are unglamorous and effective: fixed shop fixtures, flood lamps, drop lights, and bright flashlights for local coverage. Position matters as much as wattage — place lamps to kill glare off machined surfaces, and let low-angle light rake the surface when an indication needs contrast.

White light keeps a second job even in fluorescent shops: pre-cleaning checks, surface-condition review, and any evaluation the procedure requires under visible conditions. A calibrated white-light reading belongs in the record either way.

sections.caption: Visible inspection requires at least 100 fc of white light at the surface, while fluorescent inspection pairs a minimum of 1,000 µW/cm² of UV-A with ambient light held at or below 2 fc — all verified at the examination surface.

sections.heading: UV-A Sources
sections.body:
Fluorescent particles flip the logic: darken the room and pour energy into the particles instead. The energy is **UV-A** — long-wave ultraviolet from roughly **320 to 400 nm, centered near 365 nm** — which the particle dye absorbs and re-emits as yellow-green light, right where the dark-adapted eye is most sensitive.

The working requirement: **at least 1,000 µW/cm² of UV-A intensity at the examination surface**, verified with a radiometer.

Sources you will meet:

- **Mercury-vapor spot lamps** — the classic 100 W black light. A deep-violet filter passes UV-A while blocking visible light and the harsher short wavelengths. They need **several minutes of warm-up** to reach full output, drift with line voltage, and after a power interruption must cool before restriking — so verification happens after stabilization.
- **LED UV-A lamps** — instant-on, cool-running, battery-friendly, and now dominant on new equipment. Intensity can be strong but uneven across the beam, so current practices require use per manufacturer certification plus checks of beam coverage as well as peak intensity.
- **Tubular UV fluorescent fixtures** — flood large areas at lower intensity; useful for general booth illumination, rarely sufficient alone for examination intensity.

Filter condition is part of the source: a **cracked, chipped, or missing filter leaks UV-B** and visible light — that lamp comes out of service on the spot.

sections.heading: Ambient Light and Dark Adaptation
sections.body:
Fluorescent contrast is a ratio: indication glow over background light. The glow side is fixed by your lamp and particles, so the standards squeeze the other side — **ambient visible light in the viewing area is limited, commonly to 2 fc (about 20 lux) or less**, measured at the examination surface with the UV-A source off or shielded. In practice that is a curtained booth, a hooded bench, or a tented field setup after dark — genuinely dark, not merely dim.

Your eyes are instruments too. Stepping from shop light into the booth, the eye needs time before it can detect faint indications: procedures commonly require **at least 1 minute of dark adaptation** before evaluating, and experienced inspectors give critical work longer — several minutes buys real sensitivity. Photochromic lenses that have not cleared yet delay adaptation further, one reason UV-blocking (not sunglass-dark) eyewear is the standard choice.

Guard the darkness while you work: a propped door, a white-light leak from an instrument lamp, or a phone screen can erase a faint indication mid-evaluation.

sections.caption: Typical lighting values for MT — your written procedure governs.
rows: Examination surface, UV off or shielded
Shift start / every 8 h; bulb change; power interruption

sections.heading: Measurement Devices
sections.body:
Two instruments back every lighting claim in your report.

- The **UV-A radiometer** reads irradiance in µW/cm². Its sensor is filtered to respond to the UV-A band and is placed **at the examination surface, facing the lamp, at the working distance and position** — a traditional checkpoint is verifying the lamp delivers its minimum at 15 in., with the distance actually used governing.
- The **white-light photometer** reads illuminance in footcandles or lux — for both the ≥ 100 fc visible-method check and the ≤ 2 fc ambient check (UV source off or shielded so fluorescence around the sensor does not bias the reading).

Combination meters package both sensors. Either way, the instruments are **calibrated on a schedule** — six-month intervals are common — against traceable references; a meter with an expired sticker proves nothing.

When to measure: **at setup and the start of each shift (or every 8 hours)**, after any bulb or lamp change, after the lamp is moved or repositioned, after a power interruption (mercury lamps must restabilize), and whenever degraded performance is suspected. A failed reading stops fluorescent evaluation until the cause — aging bulb, dirty filter or reflector, low line voltage, wrong distance — is corrected and a passing reading is recorded.

sections.caption: The UV-A band sits between the filtered-out short ultraviolet wavelengths and visible light, and a radiometer at the working distance verifies the lamp delivers at least 1,000 µW/cm² at the surface.

sections.title: UV-A safety practice
sections.body: UV-A is the gentlest ultraviolet band — **while the lamp is intact**. The filter blocks the hazardous shorter wavelengths underneath; a cracked or missing filter leaks UV-B, so a damaged lamp comes out of service immediately. Never look into any UV source or aim it at people. Cover skin under strong beams for extended work — sleeves and gloves, the same respect you give sunlight. Stray UV makes the fluid of your own eye fluoresce into a milky haze; **UV-absorbing spectacles** block it, cut the haze, and actually sharpen indication contrast. Mercury lamps run hot — handle housings and bulbs accordingly — and all lamps, cords, and connections must stay sound in the wet, grounded environment around an MT bench.

figures.title: Lighting requirements: visible vs fluorescent inspection
UV-A band and the radiometer check

checkQuestions.question: The minimum UV-A intensity for fluorescent MT examination is typically:
options: 1,000 µW/cm² at the examination surface
100 µW/cm² at the lamp face
2,000 lux measured at the booth wall
10 µW/cm² anywhere inside the booth
checkQuestions.explanation: Common practice requires at least 1,000 microwatts per square centimeter of UV-A, measured with a calibrated radiometer at the examination surface — not at the lamp, and not at an arbitrary point in the booth.

checkQuestions.question: Ambient visible light during fluorescent viewing is commonly limited to:
options: 100 fc, the same as visible inspection
2 fc (about 20 lux) at the examination surface
50 lux measured at the lamp
No limit applies if the UV-A intensity is high enough
checkQuestions.explanation: Fluorescent contrast collapses as background light rises, so the viewing area is held to about 2 footcandles or less at the surface — a genuinely darkened booth or hood — regardless of how strong the UV source is.

checkQuestions.question: For visible (non-fluorescent) particle examination, the typical minimum illumination is:
options: 10 fc of any available light
1,000 µW/cm² of UV-A at the surface
100 fc (about 1,000 lux) of white light at the surface, verified with a calibrated photometer
2 fc, to preserve particle contrast
checkQuestions.explanation: Visible particles work by color contrast under white light, and the common minimum is 100 footcandles at the examination surface — confirmed by measurement, because overhead shop lighting frequently falls short at the part.

checkQuestions.question: A mercury-vapor UV lamp restrikes after a brief power dip. Before resuming fluorescent evaluation you should:
options: Begin evaluating immediately — output is highest right at startup
Allow several minutes for the lamp to restabilize, then verify intensity with a radiometer
Replace the bulb, since any power dip destroys it
Use the lamp only for general booth lighting from then on
checkQuestions.explanation: Mercury-vapor lamps need minutes to warm to full output and must cool before restriking; readings only count after stabilization. A power interruption is a standard trigger for re-verification with the radiometer.

checkQuestions.question: During checks you find a chip in the deep-violet filter of a mercury-vapor UV lamp, though intensity still reads above minimum. The lamp:
options: May be used since intensity still exceeds 1,000 µW/cm²
May be used for non-critical work only
Comes out of service immediately — a damaged filter can leak hazardous UV-B and visible light
Needs only to be mounted farther from the inspector
checkQuestions.explanation: The filter is what confines the output to UV-A; a crack or chip leaks shorter-wavelength ultraviolet that injures eyes and skin, and the stray visible light degrades contrast. Intensity passing does not make a damaged lamp safe.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §4.6 (light sources, measurement devices, safety)
Practices such as ASTM E709/E1444 and ASME Section V Article 7 specify lighting minimums and verification (generic reference)
Typical values: UV-A ≥ 1,000 µW/cm² at the surface; ambient ≤ 2 fc (≈ 20 lux); white light ≥ 100 fc (≈ 1,000 lux)
